headline: Shift Energy Costs to Large Users
short_summary: This bill aims to shift certain electricity costs, such as capacity auction and distribution line costs, from residential customers to large energy users like data centers, potentially lowering residential bills.
status: Last Action: Governor's recommendation received by House on 2026-04-13
supporters: Senator L. Louise Lucas, Del. Destiny LeVere Bolling, residential customers, environmental advocates.
opponents: Governor Spanberger (due to amendments weakening the cost shift), some critics of the governor's amendments.
news_headline: Governor Amends Bills Shifting Data Center Costs
news_summary: Governor Spanberger proposed amendments to HB 1393 and SB 253, which aimed to shift energy costs from residential customers to large data centers, leading to criticism that her changes weakened the original intent to lower residential bills. The amendments removed explicit cost-shift mechanisms, replacing them with vague language, and also reduced funding for line undergrounding programs.

headline: Mandate Data Center Water Reporting
short_summary: This bill requires utilities providing water to data centers to report monthly water consumption data, including reclaimed water, to the State Water Control Board, enhancing transparency on resource use.
status: Last Action: Approved by Governor-Chapter 896 (Effective 1/1/2027) on 2026-04-13
supporters: Senator Kannan Srinivasan, Del. Liz Guzman, environmental advocates, communities concerned about water usage.
opponents: Data center industry (implied by previous lack of transparency).
news_headline: Virginia Enacts Data Center Water Reporting Law
news_summary: Governor Spanberger approved SB 553, a bill requiring data centers to report their water consumption to the State Water Control Board, aiming to provide greater transparency on resource use amidst growing concerns over data center impacts. This measure, effective January 1, 2027, is seen by supporters as a crucial step towards better understanding and managing the industry's environmental footprint.

headline: Regulate Data Center Siting and Noise
short_summary: This bill allows localities to require site assessments for new high energy use facilities, including data centers, to evaluate impacts on noise, water, agriculture, parks, historic sites, and forestland.
status: Last Action: Incorporated by Counties, Cities and Towns (HB153-Thomas) (Voice Vote) on 2026-01-23
supporters: Del. Josh Thomas, Senator Danica Roem (for similar SB 94), local governments, environmental advocates, residents concerned about noise and siting.
opponents: Data center developers (implied by increased regulatory burden).
news_headline: Virginia Lawmakers Pass Data Center Siting Bill
news_summary: HB 153, which allows localities to require site assessments for data centers regarding noise and environmental impacts, was a key focus of the 2026 legislative session. This bill aims to give local governments more tools to manage the rapid growth of data centers and address community concerns about their proximity to residential areas and natural resources.
